items.correct: A sustained rise in the general price level over time — reducing the purchasing power of money. Measured by the percentage change in a price index.
items.misconception: Inflation is when prices are high.
items.random_wrong: Inflation is when the economy grows too fast.
items.why: Inflation is about RISING prices, not high prices. A country can have stable high prices (no inflation) or rising low prices (high inflation). Students confuse level with rate of change.

items.q: What does price elasticity of demand measure?
items.correct: The responsiveness of quantity demanded to a change in price — the percentage change in quantity divided by the percentage change in price.
items.misconception: PED measures how flexible or stretchy a product is.
items.random_wrong: PED measures whether a product is expensive or cheap.
items.why: Students take 'elasticity' literally. It is a measure of sensitivity — how much quantity responds when price changes. The product itself isn't 'elastic'; demand for it is.

items.q: What causes the supply curve to shift?
items.correct: Changes in production costs, technology, taxes/subsidies, number of producers, expectations — anything affecting supply other than the good's own price.
items.misconception: When demand rises, the supply curve shifts right to meet it.
items.random_wrong: Supply shifts only when consumers want more.
items.why: Rising demand causes a movement along the supply curve, not a shift of it. Supply shifts only when something on the supply side changes. Students entangle the two curves.

items.q: Which is an example of EXPANSIONARY monetary policy?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: Cutting the Bank Rate. “Raising income tax” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Rising government borrowing pushes interest rates up, reducing private investment. This effect is called?
items.why: It's Crowding out. “Multiplier” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Government spends £20bn extra. MPC = 0.8. Multiplier effect raises GDP by?
items.why: The real figure is 100. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
